text: laparoscopic primary repair of perforated diverticulitis by Dr. Andrew Iraniha.

This is a fifty-two-year-old female who presented with two days history of severe left lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting and fever and found to have sigmoid diverticulitis with microperforation on her CT scan. Despite conservative therapy with bowel rest and antibiotics, she continued to have increasing abdominal pain and tenderness.
During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes the sigmoid colon affected by diverticulitis, with an intraabdominal abscess visible but no gross fecal contamination present.
The video shows the laparoscopic primary repair of perforated diverticulitis, including the drainage of an intraabdominal abscess. The procedure aims to avoid the need for a temporary colostomy.

The laparoscopic inspection reveals significant sigmoid diverticulitis with pericolonic inflammatory adhesions, a small interloop abscess, and frank intraabdominal purulent fluid. The surgeon drains the purulent fluid and irrigates the abdomen with an antibiotic solution, noting a microperforation of the sigmoid diverticulitis without gross fecal contamination.

The surgeon performs a primary repair of the perforated diverticulitis using intracorporeal suturing with multiple intracranial sutures and a two-hole etabond technique, aiming to eliminate the need for a temporary colostomy.

Even though a sigmoid colectomy with temporary colostomy is the standard of care in these emergency situations, these patients would require second operation to take down their colostomy and reconstruct their colon after a minimum of three months. However, in this case, to prevent a temporary colostomy and improve the patient quality of life postoperatively, it was decided to repair the microperforation primarily and drain the abscess.

This patient tolerated the procedure very well and was discharged home on day number three after the operation with two antibiotics. Three months later, she underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis. This approach did eliminate the requirement for creation of a temporary colostomy and improved the patient's quality of life tremendously within that three months interval.

Obviously, this surgical option would not be suitable in most emergency cases when there's gross contamination or when patients are extremely sick and unstable. However, in selected cases, it could be the alternative surgical option.
